title: Job curses his birthday
summary_text: Job pours out his grief and curses the day of his birth. He wishes the night of his conception had been blotted out and asks why life was given to a man who cannot find rest. The chapter is a raw lament, but it stays honest before God instead of turning away from him.

title: Bildad describes the fate of the wicked
summary_text: Bildad answers with a hard speech about the fate of the wicked. He assumes that Job's suffering must prove hidden guilt, and he describes ruin in stark, terrifying images. The chapter shows how moral certainty can become cruelty when it refuses to listen. Its verdict is severe, but it does not settle the question of Job's innocence.
key_points: Bildad begins by rebuking Job for hunting for words.
He insists that the light of the wicked will be put out.
He describes terror, traps, and destruction as the wicked man's future.
Job is treated as if his suffering proves guilt.
The speech gives no comfort and no real answer to Job's case.

Surely the light of the wicked shall be put out.

Surely such are the dwellings of the unrighteous.

title: Job remembers former honor
summary_text: Job remembers the season when God's favor made his life full of honor, usefulness, and respect. He describes how people listened, blessed him, and sought his counsel. The memory sharpens the contrast with his present humiliation, but it also shows the kind of life he once lived. The chapter is a lament over lost honor, not a boast.
key_points: Job recalls the days when God's lamp shone over his head.
He remembers being sought out for counsel and justice.
He was honored by the young, the great, and the needy alike.
He became a father to the poor and a helper of the widow.
The chapter contrasts past honor with his present suffering.

title: The Lord displays his creatures
summary_text: Knowest thou the time when the wild goats of the rock bring forth. The Lord answers from the whirlwind and uses questions to expose human limits. Creation, creatures, and power all point beyond Job's smallness. And where the slain are, there is she.
key_points: Knowest thou the time when the wild goats of the rock bring forth.
Or canst thou mark when the hinds do calve.
Canst thou number the months that they fulfil.
Or knowest thou the time when they bring forth.
They bow themselves, they bring forth their young, They cast out their pains.

title: Leviathan and the Lord's unmatched power
summary_text: Canst thou draw out leviathan with a fishhook. The Lord answers from the whirlwind and uses questions to expose human limits. Creation, creatures, and power all point beyond Job's smallness. He is king over all the sons of pride.

title: Job repents and is restored
summary_text: Then Job answered Jehovah, and said. Job humbles himself before God's rebuke, prays for his friends, and sees the friends' fortunes restored. The chapter closes with renewed blessing and the record of a long life. So Job died, being old and full of days.
key_points: Then Job answered Jehovah, and said.
I abhor myself, and repent in dust and ashes.
Jehovah turned the captivity of Job, when he prayed for his friends.
Jehovah gave Job twice as much as he had before.
So Job died, being old and full of days.
